steps.title: Week 2 Closing: Safety Built
steps.body:
Look at what you did this week.

You named the Four Horsemen — the patterns that predicted your relationship's erosion. You learned the antidotes and practiced the Gentle Startup. You explored vulnerability and learned why armor costs you the closeness it promises to protect.

You mapped the Emotional Bank Account and identified your deposit and withdrawal habits. You learned to fight fair — complaints not criticisms, productive timeouts, repair attempts. And yesterday, you learned to actually listen.

That's not a list of concepts. That's a new set of tools — tools that change how a marriage feels from the inside.

Today, we close Week 2 with the deepest kind of sharing.

In 1997, psychologist Arthur Aron published a study on accelerated intimacy.

His finding: mutual vulnerability — structured, reciprocal sharing of progressively deeper questions — creates genuine closeness between strangers in under an hour.

The paper became famous as 'The 36 Questions That Lead to Love.'

The questions work on strangers. They work even better on couples who've been together for years — because you'll discover that some of the answers have changed.
highlights: some of the answers have changed

steps.title: Why Questions Build Intimacy
steps.body:
Most couples stop asking each other deep questions after the first year or two.

You think you know each other. You know the facts — schedules, preferences, history. But when was the last time you asked:

What are you most proud of? What do you secretly regret? What's a dream you've never said out loud? What's something about yourself you're still figuring out?

People change. Your partner is not the same person they were five years ago. Neither are you.

Deep questions create fresh seeing. And fresh seeing creates the same feeling that first drew you together — the sense of encountering someone genuinely interesting who wants to know you back.

steps.title: The Rules for Deep Sharing
steps.body:
For this exercise to work, a few ground rules:

1. No judgment. Whatever they say, receive it with curiosity, not evaluation.

2. Answer the questions too. This is mutual. It's not an interview.

3. No phones. One hour of real presence.

4. You don't have to do all the questions. Pick the ones that feel right. Depth is the goal, not completion.

5. If something comes up that surprises you — about them or about yourself — name it. 'I didn't know that about you.' 'I've never said that to anyone before.'

Those moments are the point.

steps.title: Question 1: The Dinner Guests
steps.prompt:
If you could invite anyone in the world — living or dead — to dinner, who would it be and why?

Answer for yourself first. Then imagine your partner's answer.

Will you ask them tonight?

steps.title: Question 2: What You're Grateful For
steps.prompt:
Name three things in your life that you feel genuinely grateful for — and try to be specific about why each one matters to you, not just what it is.

Include things beyond the obvious. Something small that matters more than it should. Something you're afraid you take for granted.

steps.title: Question 3: A Perfect Day
steps.prompt:
Describe your perfect day — from the moment you wake up to the moment you fall asleep. Include where you are, who's with you, what you're doing and feeling.

Don't make it practical. Make it true.

steps.title: When the Questions Get Hard
steps.body:
Some of these questions will surface things that are uncomfortable. Old longings. Unspoken fears. Dreams that didn't happen the way you hoped.

That's not a problem. That's the point.

If a question surfaces a feeling — name it. 'That one hit something I didn't expect.' You don't have to know why. You just have to stay in the room with it instead of deflecting.

The couples who do this exercise and go deeper than they planned aren't the ones with the least to process. They're the ones who are willing to be seen anyway.

steps.title: Question 4: Your Life Story (Short Version)
steps.prompt:
In four minutes or less, tell your life story — as many chapters as you can. Include the pivotal moments, the turns you didn't expect, the things that shaped who you are.

Write it here first. Then share it with your partner — and ask for theirs.

steps.title: Question 5: What I Never Say
steps.prompt:
Name one thing you wish your partner understood about you that you've never found the right way to say.

Not a complaint. Not a criticism. Something about who you are — a fear, a need, a way you experience the world — that feels important and unsaid.

steps.title: Question 6: What You Love About Them
steps.prompt:
Name 5 things you genuinely love or admire about your partner. Not their role ('great parent'). Their person.

Things like: the way they laugh. How they handle pressure. A quality you've noticed that they don't see in themselves. Something they do when they think nobody's watching.

Write it. Then say it to them.

steps.title: Question 7: A Dream You Haven't Said Out Loud
steps.prompt:
Is there something you've wanted — a life, a version of yourself, a path not taken — that you've never fully said out loud to your partner?

Not a complaint about your current life. A genuine dream or desire that's still alive in you.

Write it here. Then decide: is this worth sharing?

steps.title: What These Questions Build
steps.body:
Arthur Aron's research found something important: it wasn't any single question that created intimacy. It was the experience of mutual, progressive, honest disclosure — combined with attentive listening.

When both people are willing to be seen and both people show up fully to witness the other — something shifts.

You move from being two people who live together to being two people who truly know each other.

That's the foundation all the other skills are built on. And it doesn't expire — you can come back to these questions any time. Pick three. Sit outside. See what emerges.

steps.title: Achievement Unlocked: Foundation Builder
steps.body:
You completed Week 2 — Safety. You learned what destroys emotional safety and spent seven days building the skills to restore it. You practiced vulnerability, listening, and honest sharing.

That's not theory anymore. That's a lived experience.

Badge earned: Foundation Builder

steps.title: Week 3 Begins Tomorrow
steps.body:
You've built safety. Next week is about connection — deepening the friendship, intimacy, and joy at the center of your relationship.

Two weeks down. The work you've done this week will be felt for years.

See you tomorrow.

steps.title: Keep the Conversations Going
steps.body:
Set a reminder to try the deep questions exercise with your partner this week. Pick 3–5 questions and make an evening of it.

The best time: no phones, no kids, no agenda. Just two people who chose each other, trying to keep knowing each other.

steps.title: Week 2 Complete
steps.body:
Safety is built one honest moment at a time.

You've had a lot of those moments this week. Keep going.
